  • Hide All Code

Module 05: Latent Class and Latent Profile Analysis

Computational Social Science — Graduate & Undergraduate

2026-04-04

1 Overview: When Distributions Are the Wrong Frame

Standard quantitative analysis is variable-centred: it asks how variables relate to each other across a population. Education predicts income; social trust predicts civic participation; conservative ideology predicts political disengagement. The assumption is that these relationships hold, in roughly the same way, for everyone.

Person-centred analysis challenges this assumption. It asks: are there qualitatively distinct types of people for whom these relationships look fundamentally different? Rather than finding the average relationship between political identity and media consumption across all respondents, it asks whether there are discrete types — political identity profiles — each with their own characteristic media behaviour.

This distinction matters. Average relationships can mask deep heterogeneity. The average correlation between social media use and depression may be near zero even if there is a small group for whom the relationship is very strong, and a larger group for whom it is slightly positive, and a larger group still for whom it is essentially absent. A typological analysis finds those groups; a variable-centred analysis averages over them.

Learning goals:

  • Explain the conceptual difference between variable-centred and person-centred analysis
  • Run a complete LCA workflow: data preparation, model fitting, selection, interpretation
  • Read the full poLCA output section by section
  • Assess class quality using posterior probabilities, entropy, and classification certainty
  • Interpret class profiles and name classes substantively
  • Run LPA on continuous indicators using tidyLPA
  • Connect class membership to external predictors and outcomes

2 Part A: Latent Class Analysis

2.1 The Conceptual Model

LCA assumes the population consists of \(K\) mutually exclusive, exhaustive latent classes. Within each class, the observed items are locally independent — uncorrelated with each other. All item correlations in the full sample arise because different classes respond differently to different items.

For a binary item \(Y_j\) and class \(k\): \[P(Y_j = 1 \mid \text{Class} = k) = \rho_{jk}\]

The \(\rho_{jk}\) values are the item response probabilities — the probability that a member of class \(k\) gives a “Yes” response to item \(j\). These are the core output you interpret to understand what each class means.

What a latent class is not: It is not a group defined by a threshold on a single variable. It is defined by a pattern across multiple variables simultaneously. A person belongs to a class because their overall response pattern matches the class’s characteristic profile, not because they scored high or low on one item.

2.2 The cheating Dataset

We use cheating from poLCA: 319 undergraduates responding to four binary questions about academic dishonesty.

data("cheating")

cat("=== cheating Dataset ===\n")
## === cheating Dataset ===
cat("N:", nrow(cheating), "respondents\n\n")
## N: 319 respondents
cat("Items (1=No, 2=Yes in poLCA coding):\n")
## Items (1=No, 2=Yes in poLCA coding):
tibble(
  Item = c("LIEEXAM","LIEPAPER","FRAUD","COPYEXAM"),
  Question = c(
    "Told instructor missed exam for false reason",
    "Told instructor missed paper deadline falsely",
    "Turned in work done by someone else",
    "Copied from another student during exam"
  )
) |>
  kbl() |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options=c("striped","hover"), full_width=FALSE)
Item Question
LIEEXAM Told instructor missed exam for false reason
LIEPAPER Told instructor missed paper deadline falsely
FRAUD Turned in work done by someone else
COPYEXAM Copied from another student during exam 
cat("\nMarginal frequencies (% responding Yes = category 2):\n")
## 
## Marginal frequencies (% responding Yes = category 2):
cheating |>
  summarise(across(c(LIEEXAM,LIEPAPER,FRAUD,COPYEXAM),
                   ~round(mean(.x==2)*100,1))) |>
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to="Item", values_to="Pct_Yes") |>
  kbl(col.names=c("Item","% Yes")) |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options=c("striped","condensed"), full_width=FALSE)
Item % Yes
LIEEXAM 10.7
LIEPAPER 11.9
FRAUD 6.6
COPYEXAM 21.3

Why these marginal rates matter: Before running LCA, understand what the data looks like on each item separately. Items with very low (< 5%) or very high (> 95%) marginal rates carry little information for class separation — almost everyone gives the same response, so the item cannot differentiate classes. Items in the 20–80% range are most informative.

2.3 Fitting Multiple Models

poLCA requires items coded as integers starting at 1. For binary items: 1 = No, 2 = Yes.

CRITICAL: Always use nrep (multiple random starts). LCA optimisation can converge to local maxima — incorrect solutions that look right but are not the global optimum. Run the model from many random starting values and keep the solution with the highest log-likelihood.

set.seed(2024)

# Define the formula: all four items on the right, 1 on the left (no covariates yet)
f_cheat <- cbind(LIEEXAM, LIEPAPER, FRAUD, COPYEXAM) ~ 1

# Fit K=1 to K=4 models
fit_all <- map_dfr(1:4, function(k) {
  # nrep=10: fit from 10 random starts, keep best solution
  fits <- map(1:10, ~poLCA(f_cheat, cheating, nclass=k,
                            maxiter=2000, verbose=FALSE))
  # Keep the run with the highest log-likelihood (best solution)
  best  <- fits[[which.max(sapply(fits, `[[`, "llik"))]]

  # Compute entropy: measure of classification certainty (0=uncertain, 1=certain)
  pp    <- best$posterior
  # Entropy formula: 1 - (average cross-entropy / log K)
  ent   <- if (k == 1) NA_real_ else {
    1 - (-sum(pp * log(pp + 1e-10)) / (nrow(pp) * log(ncol(pp))))
  }

  tibble(
    K       = k,
    LogLik  = round(best$llik, 2),
    AIC     = round(best$aic, 2),
    BIC     = round(best$bic, 2),
    Npar    = best$npar,
    Entropy = round(ent, 3),
    Min_class_pct = if (k==1) 100 else round(min(best$P)*100, 1)
  )
})

kbl(fit_all,
    caption = "LCA Model Fit: K = 1–4 Classes (cheating dataset)",
    col.names = c("K","Log-Lik","AIC","BIC","# Params","Entropy","Min class %")) |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options=c("striped","hover")) |>
  row_spec(which.min(fit_all$BIC), bold=TRUE, background="#EAFAF1") |>
  footnote(general=paste(
    "Bold/green row = BIC-preferred solution.",
    "\nEntropy: 0 = classes perfectly overlap (useless), 1 = perfect separation.",
    "\nEntropy > .80 is generally considered good classification certainty.",
    "\nMin class % = size of the smallest class. Classes < 5% may be noise."
  ))
LCA Model Fit: K = 1–4 Classes (cheating dataset)
K Log-Lik AIC BIC # Params Entropy Min class %
1 -467.44 942.88 957.94 4 NA 100.0
2 -440.03 898.05 931.94 9 0.737 16.1
3 -436.24 900.47 953.18 14 0.840 3.5
4 -436.15 910.29 981.83 19 0.566 5.5
Note:
Bold/green row = BIC-preferred solution.
Entropy: 0 = classes perfectly overlap (useless), 1 = perfect separation.
Entropy > .80 is generally considered good classification certainty.
Min class % = size of the smallest class. Classes < 5% may be noise.

2.4 Reading the Model Selection Table

How to Read the Model Selection Table
Criterion Direction What It Measures
Log-Likelihood Higher = better Overall model fit. Increases with every additional class — always check against BIC to see if the improvement justifies the complexity.
AIC Lower = better AIC penalises for number of parameters. Tends to favour more classes than BIC. Use as secondary criterion.
BIC Lower = better BIC penalises more heavily for parameters. Preferred criterion in social science for class enumeration. Find the ‘elbow’ or minimum.
Entropy Higher = better (> .80 good) Classification certainty: how unambiguously each respondent is assigned to a class. Does not indicate ‘correct’ K — use alongside BIC.
Min class % Should be > 5% Smallest class as % of sample. Very small classes (< 5%) may be noise or artefacts. Verify they are theoretically interpretable.

2.5 Running and Reading the Best Model

best_k <- fit_all$K[which.min(fit_all$BIC)]
cat("BIC-optimal K:", best_k, "\n\n")
## BIC-optimal K: 2
set.seed(42)
lca <- poLCA(f_cheat, cheating, nclass=best_k,
             maxiter=2000, verbose=TRUE, nrep=10, graphs=FALSE)
## Model 1: llik = -440.0271 ... best llik = -440.0271
## Model 2: llik = -440.0271 ... best llik = -440.0271
## Model 3: llik = -440.0271 ... best llik = -440.0271
## Model 4: llik = -440.0271 ... best llik = -440.0271
## Model 5: llik = -440.0271 ... best llik = -440.0271
## Model 6: llik = -440.0271 ... best llik = -440.0271
## Model 7: llik = -440.0271 ... best llik = -440.0271
## Model 8: llik = -440.0271 ... best llik = -440.0271
## Model 9: llik = -440.0271 ... best llik = -440.0271
## Model 10: llik = -440.0271 ... best llik = -440.0271
## Conditional item response (column) probabilities,
##  by outcome variable, for each class (row) 
##  
## $LIEEXAM
##            Pr(1)  Pr(2)
## class 1:  0.9834 0.0166
## class 2:  0.4231 0.5769
## 
## $LIEPAPER
##            Pr(1)  Pr(2)
## class 1:  0.9708 0.0292
## class 2:  0.4109 0.5891
## 
## $FRAUD
##            Pr(1)  Pr(2)
## class 1:  0.9629 0.0371
## class 2:  0.7839 0.2161
## 
## $COPYEXAM
##            Pr(1)  Pr(2)
## class 1:  0.8180 0.1820
## class 2:  0.6236 0.3764
## 
## Estimated class population shares 
##  0.8394 0.1606 
##  
## Predicted class memberships (by modal posterior prob.) 
##  0.8307 0.1693 
##  
## ========================================================= 
## Fit for 2 latent classes: 
## ========================================================= 
## number of observations: 319 
## number of estimated parameters: 9 
## residual degrees of freedom: 6 
## maximum log-likelihood: -440.0271 
##  
## AIC(2): 898.0542
## BIC(2): 931.9409
## G^2(2): 7.764242 (Likelihood ratio/deviance statistic) 
## X^2(2): 8.323451 (Chi-square goodness of fit) 
##

Reading the verbose poLCA output during estimation:

When you set verbose=TRUE, poLCA prints iteration-by-iteration information. You will see: - Iteration number and log-likelihood: The log-likelihood should increase (become less negative) with each iteration. If it stops improving, the algorithm has converged. - “EM algorithm terminated at iteration X”: Normal convergence message. - Multiple runs: With nrep=10, poLCA runs the model 10 times and prints results for each. The final solution shown is the best (highest log-likelihood).

# Print the full model object
print(lca)
## Conditional item response (column) probabilities,
##  by outcome variable, for each class (row) 
##  
## $LIEEXAM
##            Pr(1)  Pr(2)
## class 1:  0.9834 0.0166
## class 2:  0.4231 0.5769
## 
## $LIEPAPER
##            Pr(1)  Pr(2)
## class 1:  0.9708 0.0292
## class 2:  0.4109 0.5891
## 
## $FRAUD
##            Pr(1)  Pr(2)
## class 1:  0.9629 0.0371
## class 2:  0.7839 0.2161
## 
## $COPYEXAM
##            Pr(1)  Pr(2)
## class 1:  0.8180 0.1820
## class 2:  0.6236 0.3764
## 
## Estimated class population shares 
##  0.8394 0.1606 
##  
## Predicted class memberships (by modal posterior prob.) 
##  0.8307 0.1693 
##  
## ========================================================= 
## Fit for 2 latent classes: 
## ========================================================= 
## number of observations: 319 
## number of estimated parameters: 9 
## residual degrees of freedom: 6 
## maximum log-likelihood: -440.0271 
##  
## AIC(2): 898.0542
## BIC(2): 931.9409
## G^2(2): 7.764242 (Likelihood ratio/deviance statistic) 
## X^2(2): 8.323451 (Chi-square goodness of fit) 
##

2.6 Reading the Full poLCA Output — Section by Section

Complete Guide to poLCA Print Output
Output Section What It Means
Model: cbind(…)~1 Confirms the formula. The ‘~1’ means no covariates predicting class membership.
Number of observations: N Total respondents used (after listwise deletion).
Number of estimated parameters: P Number of free parameters. Check this matches your expectation.
Residual degrees of freedom N - P - 1. More residual df = more parsimonious model.
Maximum log-likelihood: L The log-likelihood at convergence. Higher (less negative) = better fit. Use to compare nested models.
AIC and BIC values Information criteria. Lower = better. Use BIC to compare models of different K.
Class 1 (size): Proportion of the population estimated to belong to this class (the mixing weight).
Pr(1) and Pr(2) Pr(1) = P(Y=No | Class k). Pr(2) = P(Y=Yes | Class k). These define the class profile.
Estimated class population shares The Pi vector: estimated population proportions for each class.
Predicted class memberships (by modal) Count of respondents assigned to each class by their modal posterior probability.
Conditional item response probabilities The rho matrix: P(Y=j | Class=k) for each item j and class k. This is what you interpret.

2.7 Extracting and Interpreting Class Profiles

# Class sizes (population proportions)
cat("=== CLASS SIZES ===\n")
## === CLASS SIZES ===
cat("These are the estimated population proportions Pi:\n\n")
## These are the estimated population proportions Pi:
sizes <- round(lca$P * 100, 1)
for (k in seq_along(sizes)) {
  cat("  Class", k, ":", sizes[k], "% of population\n")
}
##   Class 1 : 83.9 % of population
##   Class 2 : 16.1 % of population
cat("\n=== ITEM RESPONSE PROBABILITIES (Rho) ===\n")
## 
## === ITEM RESPONSE PROBABILITIES (Rho) ===
cat("P(Yes | Class k) for each item × class combination:\n\n")
## P(Yes | Class k) for each item × class combination:
# Extract P(Yes) = P(category 2) for each item × class
rho_tbl <- map_dfr(seq_along(lca$probs), function(j) {
  item_name <- names(lca$probs)[j]
  probs_mat <- lca$probs[[j]]  # rows = classes, cols = categories (1=No, 2=Yes)
  tibble(
    Item  = item_name,
    Class = paste("Class", seq_len(nrow(probs_mat))),
    P_No  = round(probs_mat[, 1], 3),
    P_Yes = round(probs_mat[, 2], 3)
  )
})

rho_tbl |>
  dplyr::select(Item, Class, P_Yes) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from=Class, values_from=P_Yes) |>
  kbl(caption="Conditional Response Probabilities P(Yes | Class) — cheating LCA",
      col.names=c("Item", paste("Class", 1:best_k, paste0("(", sizes, "%)")))) |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options=c("striped","hover")) |>
  footnote(general=paste(
    "Each cell = probability of 'Yes' for a member of that class.",
    "Read: 'If someone is in Class 2, they have an X% chance of having lied about an exam.'",
    "\nHigher values = this class is more likely to have engaged in this behaviour."
  ))
Conditional Response Probabilities P(Yes | Class) — cheating LCA
Item Class 1 (83.9%) Class 2 (16.1%)
LIEEXAM 0.017 0.577
LIEPAPER 0.029 0.589
FRAUD 0.037 0.216
COPYEXAM 0.182 0.376
Note:
Each cell = probability of ‘Yes’ for a member of that class. Read: ‘If someone is in Class 2, they have an X% chance of having lied about an exam.’
Higher values = this class is more likely to have engaged in this behaviour. 
ggplot(rho_tbl, aes(x=Item, y=P_Yes, colour=Class, group=Class)) +
  geom_line(linewidth=1.4) +
  geom_point(size=4.5) +
  geom_hline(yintercept=0.5, linetype="dashed", colour="grey60", linewidth=0.7) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits=c(0,1), labels=scales::percent,
                     name="P(Yes | Class)") +
  scale_colour_manual(values=css_palette(best_k), name=NULL) +
  labs(
    title    = paste0("LCA Profile Plot: K = ", best_k, " Classes (cheating)"),
    subtitle = paste(
      "Each line = one latent class's response pattern.",
      "Dashed line at 50% = chance level.",
      "\nLines far apart = classes well separated. Lines overlapping = similar classes (consider fewer K)."
    ),
    x = "Academic dishonesty item",
    caption = paste("Class sizes:", paste(sizes, "%", collapse=", "),
                    ". Source: poLCA::cheating.")
  ) +
  theme(legend.position="bottom")

Naming the classes — the most important interpretive step:

Look at each class’s profile and ask: “What kind of person is this?” The class name should reflect the pattern across items, not just the overall level.

  • A class with high P(Yes) on all items = “Serial cheaters” (engaged in multiple forms)
  • A class with low P(Yes) on all items = “Academic integrity group” (rarely cheat)
  • A class with high P(Yes) on lie items but low on fraud/copying = “Excuse-givers” (lie about absences but don’t commit academic fraud)

Never use algorithmic labels like “Class 1” or “High group” in a publication. Interpretive labels like these above communicate substantive meaning.

2.8 Posterior Probabilities and Classification Quality

Every respondent gets a posterior probability of belonging to each class. These probabilities sum to 1 across classes. The posterior probability for class \(k\) given response pattern \(\mathbf{y}\):

\[P(C=k \mid \mathbf{y}) \propto \pi_k \prod_j \rho_{jk}^{y_j}(1-\rho_{jk})^{1-y_j}\]

# lca$posterior is an N × K matrix of posterior probabilities
cat("=== POSTERIOR PROBABILITY MATRIX (first 10 rows) ===\n")
## === POSTERIOR PROBABILITY MATRIX (first 10 rows) ===
cat("Rows = respondents, Columns = classes\n")
## Rows = respondents, Columns = classes
cat("Each row sums to 1.0\n\n")
## Each row sums to 1.0
round(head(lca$posterior, 10), 3) |> print()
##        [,1]  [,2]
##  [1,] 0.979 0.021
##  [2,] 0.979 0.021
##  [3,] 0.979 0.021
##  [4,] 0.979 0.021
##  [5,] 0.979 0.021
##  [6,] 0.979 0.021
##  [7,] 0.979 0.021
##  [8,] 0.979 0.021
##  [9,] 0.979 0.021
## [10,] 0.979 0.021
cat("\nRow sums (should all be 1.0):", round(rowSums(head(lca$posterior,5)), 6), "\n")
## 
## Row sums (should all be 1.0): 1 1 1 1 1
# Assess classification quality
classified <- cheating |>
  mutate(
    modal_class  = factor(lca$predclass, labels=paste("Class", 1:best_k)),
    max_post     = apply(lca$posterior, 1, max),  # posterior for assigned class
    certain      = max_post >= 0.80               # "clearly classified"
  )

cat("=== CLASSIFICATION QUALITY ===\n\n")
## === CLASSIFICATION QUALITY ===
cat("Average posterior probability by class (AvePP):\n")
## Average posterior probability by class (AvePP):
classified |>
  group_by(modal_class) |>
  summarise(
    N           = n(),
    Pct_sample  = round(n()/nrow(classified)*100,1),
    AvePP       = round(mean(max_post), 3),
    Pct_certain = round(mean(certain)*100, 1),
    .groups     = "drop"
  ) |>
  kbl(col.names=c("Class","N","% Sample","Avg. Post. Prob.",
                  "% Clearly classified (PP ≥ .80)"),
      caption="Classification Quality by Latent Class") |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options=c("striped","hover")) |>
  footnote(general=paste(
    "Avg. Post. Prob. (AvePP) > .80 = good classification certainty for that class.",
    "\n% Clearly classified = respondents where we are reasonably confident about class assignment.",
    "\nLow AvePP for a class suggests that class is not well-separated from others."
  ))
Classification Quality by Latent Class
Class N % Sample Avg. Post. Prob. % Clearly classified (PP ≥ .80)
Class 1 265 83.1 0.965 98.1
Class 2 54 16.9 0.775 50.0
Note:
Avg. Post. Prob. (AvePP) > .80 = good classification certainty for that class.
% Clearly classified = respondents where we are reasonably confident about class assignment.
Low AvePP for a class suggests that class is not well-separated from others.

Why AvePP matters: If a class has AvePP = .62, respondents assigned to it are only moderately more likely to actually belong to it than to other classes. This means the class is not cleanly identified in the data — the solution with this K may be over-extracting. An AvePP of .90 means respondents are being assigned with high confidence.

2.9 Adding Covariates to Predict Class Membership

Once you have a K-class solution, you can model class membership as a function of observed predictors using a multinomial logistic regression built into poLCA:

# GPA is included in the cheating dataset as a potential predictor
# Fit the same K-class model but with GPA predicting class membership
cat("=== GPA as predictor of class membership ===\n")
## === GPA as predictor of class membership ===
cat("Does academic performance predict which class a student falls into?\n\n")
## Does academic performance predict which class a student falls into?
f_covariate <- cbind(LIEEXAM, LIEPAPER, FRAUD, COPYEXAM) ~ GPA

set.seed(42)
lca_cov <- poLCA(f_covariate, cheating, nclass=best_k,
                 maxiter=2000, verbose=FALSE, nrep=5, graphs=FALSE)

cat("Multinomial logit coefficients (predicting class membership from GPA):\n")
## Multinomial logit coefficients (predicting class membership from GPA):
# Reference class is the last class by default
cat("Reference class:", best_k, "\n\n")
## Reference class: 2
lca_cov$coeff |> round(3) |> print()
##               [,1]
## (Intercept) -0.113
## GPA          0.842
cat("\nInterpretation: A positive coefficient means higher GPA increases the\n")
## 
## Interpretation: A positive coefficient means higher GPA increases the
cat("log-odds of being in that class (vs. the reference class).\n")
## log-odds of being in that class (vs. the reference class).
# Predicted class probabilities across GPA values
gpa_range <- seq(min(cheating$GPA, na.rm=TRUE),
                 max(cheating$GPA, na.rm=TRUE), length.out=100)

# Use the coeff from the model to compute predicted probabilities
# predict() not available for poLCA; compute manually via logistic coefficients
X_new   <- cbind(1, gpa_range)
logits  <- X_new %*% lca_cov$coeff
lmat    <- cbind(0, logits)
pred_new <- exp(lmat) / rowSums(exp(lmat))
colnames(pred_new) <- paste("Class", seq_len(ncol(pred_new)))

pred_df <- as.data.frame(pred_new) |>
  mutate(GPA=gpa_range) |>
  pivot_longer(-GPA, names_to="Class", values_to="Prob")

ggplot(pred_df, aes(x=GPA, y=Prob, colour=Class)) +
  geom_line(linewidth=1.3) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels=scales::percent, name="P(Class | GPA)") +
  scale_colour_manual(values=css_palette(best_k), name=NULL) +
  labs(
    title    = "Predicted Class Membership Probabilities by GPA",
    subtitle = paste("Higher GPA should predict membership in low-cheating classes.",
                     "\nLines crossing = GPA strongly differentiates classes."),
    x="GPA (academic performance)"
  ) +
  theme(legend.position="bottom")

3 Part B: Latent Profile Analysis

3.1 LCA vs. LPA: The Core Difference

Feature LCA LPA
Indicator type Categorical (binary or ordinal) Continuous (interval/ratio)
Within-class distribution Multinomial Multivariate normal
Class profile Item response probabilities Means on each indicator
Everything else Identical Identical

LPA finds groups of people with similar levels and shapes of response across continuous variables. Where LCA shows “Class 1 has P(Yes) = .82 on item A,” LPA shows “Profile 1 has mean = 72 on scale A.”

3.2 PISA Science Data

library(tidyLPA)
data(pisaUSA15)

cat("=== PISA 2015 US Science Data ===\n")
## === PISA 2015 US Science Data ===
cat("N:", nrow(pisaUSA15), "US students\n\n")
## N: 5712 US students
pisa_vars <- c("broad_interest","enjoyment","instrumental_mot","self_efficacy")
pisaUSA15 |>
  dplyr::select(all_of(pisa_vars)) |>
  skimr::skim() |>
  dplyr::select(skim_variable, n_missing, numeric.mean, numeric.sd, numeric.p0, numeric.p100) |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~round(.x,1))) |>
  kbl(col.names=c("Scale","N missing","Mean","SD","Min","Max"),
      caption="PISA 2015 US Variables") |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options=c("striped","condensed"), full_width=FALSE)
PISA 2015 US Variables
Scale N missing Mean SD Min Max
broad_interest 258 2.7 0.8 1 5
enjoyment 192 2.8 0.8 1 4
instrumental_mot 268 2.1 0.8 1 4
self_efficacy 303 2.1 0.7 1 4 
set.seed(2024)
# Model 1 = equal variances across profiles (most parsimonious, most common)
# Compare K=1 to K=5
lpa_models <- pisaUSA15 |>
  dplyr::select(all_of(pisa_vars)) |>
  na.omit() |>
  estimate_profiles(n_profiles=1:5, models=1)

lpa_fit <- get_fit(lpa_models)

lpa_fit |>
  dplyr::select(Model, Classes, LogLik, AIC, BIC, Entropy, n_min, n_max) |>
  mutate(across(c(LogLik,AIC,BIC), ~round(.x,1)),
         Entropy=round(Entropy,3),
         n_min=round(n_min,2), n_max=round(n_max,2)) |>
  kbl(caption="LPA Model Comparison (PISA 2015)",
      col.names=c("Model","K","Log-Lik","AIC","BIC","Entropy",
                  "Min profile %","Max profile %")) |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options=c("striped","hover")) |>
  row_spec(which.min(lpa_fit$BIC), bold=TRUE, background="#EAFAF1")
LPA Model Comparison (PISA 2015)
Model K Log-Lik AIC BIC Entropy Min profile % Max profile %
1 1 -23839.3 47694.7 47747.4 1.000 1.00 1.00
1 2 -22442.9 44911.8 44997.4 0.749 0.25 0.75
1 3 -22008.9 44053.7 44172.3 0.753 0.16 0.66
1 4 -22009.3 44064.6 44216.1 0.495 0.02 0.61
1 5 -20847.5 41751.1 41935.5 0.905 0.08 0.46 
# Get the best solution and plot
best_lpa <- lpa_models[[which.min(lpa_fit$BIC)]]  # get_best_solution() removed in tidyLPA 2.x

plot_profiles(best_lpa) +
  labs(
    title    = "Latent Profile Analysis: US Student Achievement Profiles (PISA 2015)",
    subtitle = paste(
      "Each line = one latent profile's mean scores across five academic domains.",
      "\nProfiles that differ in SHAPE (not just level) are most theoretically interesting."
    ),
    caption  = "Source: PISA 2015, tidyLPA package. Shading = 95% CI around each mean."
  ) +
  theme(legend.position="bottom")

Interpreting LPA profiles: Read across each line to understand the shape — which subjects are strengths, which are weaknesses. If all profiles are parallel (same shape, just higher/lower), you are recovering a general ability dimension — useful, but not as theoretically rich as profiles that show genuine cross-domain trade-offs. Look for:

  • Profiles where science is high but interest is low (“capable but disengaged”)
  • Profiles where interest is high but performance is lower (“motivated but struggling”)
  • Profiles where all academic scores are high but interest is lower (“high performers, intrinsically disengaged”)

These distinctive shapes make the typological result more informative than a simple high/low split.

4 A Complete Worked Comparison: LCA vs. Simple Grouping

To make the value of LCA concrete, compare it to simply splitting respondents at the median:

# Simple approach: split each cheating item at the median and count how many "Yes"
cheating_simple <- cheating |>
  mutate(
    cheating_sum  = (LIEEXAM==2) + (LIEPAPER==2) + (FRAUD==2) + (COPYEXAM==2),
    simple_group  = factor(ifelse(cheating_sum <= 1, "Low (0-1 behaviours)",
                                  "High (2-4 behaviours)")),
    modal_class   = factor(lca$predclass, labels=paste("Class", 1:best_k))
  )

cat("=== Simple High/Low Split ===\n")
## === Simple High/Low Split ===
table(cheating_simple$simple_group) |> print()
## 
## High (2-4 behaviours)  Low (0-1 behaviours) 
##                    36                   283
cat("\n=== LCA Classes ===\n")
## 
## === LCA Classes ===
table(cheating_simple$modal_class) |> print()
## 
## Class 1 Class 2 
##     265      54
cat("\nCrosstab: LCA class × simple group\n")
## 
## Crosstab: LCA class × simple group
table(cheating_simple$modal_class, cheating_simple$simple_group) |> print()
##          
##           High (2-4 behaviours) Low (0-1 behaviours)
##   Class 1                     5                  260
##   Class 2                    31                   23
cat("\nKey insight: LCA classes may not simply reproduce the high/low split.\n")
## 
## Key insight: LCA classes may not simply reproduce the high/low split.
cat("If Class 2 shows selective cheating (high on some items, low on others),\n")
## If Class 2 shows selective cheating (high on some items, low on others),
cat("the simple split would miss this qualitative distinction.\n")
## the simple split would miss this qualitative distinction.

5 Common Mistakes

Mistake What Goes Wrong Prevention
Using only one random start May converge to a local maximum — wrong solution Always set nrep ≥ 10 in poLCA
Selecting K based on AIC alone AIC over-extracts — too many classes Use BIC as primary; check entropy and class size
Naming classes before reading profiles carefully Labels become interpretive fiction Spend time with the rho matrix before naming
Ignoring AvePP Cannot assess classification quality Always report AvePP per class; flag classes with AvePP < .80
Modal class assignment as certain truth Ignores classification uncertainty In downstream analyses, use posterior probabilities, not modal assignment, where possible
Treating small classes (< 5%) as real types Small classes often artefacts Re-examine with K-1 if any class is < 5%

6 Check Your Understanding

1. Run LCA on the cheating data with K=2 and K=3. For each solution, report: the class sizes, the item response probabilities for all items, the entropy, and the BIC. Which solution do you prefer and why?

2. In the K=2 solution, compute the average posterior probability for each class. Interpret what these values tell you about the quality of the classification.

3. A colleague argues: “LCA is just clustering. Why not use k-means?” Describe two specific differences between LCA and k-means clustering that make LCA preferable for survey data.

4. Fit LPA on the PISA data with K=3. Name the three profiles based on their pattern of means. What would be the substantive implication if these profiles are replicated in other countries — what does it tell us about the structure of academic achievement?

5. 🟥 The local independence assumption states that within each class, items are uncorrelated. How would you detect a violation of this assumption in practice? What would a violation imply about the model, and what could you do about it?
